snippet.title: Dual operators and dual whips (dipole) make for a fun activation! US-10562 w/ KI5PDO.
snippet.description:
It was fun to have Justin, KI5PDO join me at US-10562 for an activation.  In spite of really tough conditions this time of year, we had a great time and made lots of great  contacts.  The 20m dipole performed really well.  I encourage all POTA activators to do multi op activations.  It truly is an absolute blast to have someone join you for some POTA fun. Thanks you Justin for coming along for this activation.

snippet.title: Hidden HF Antennas That Really Work
snippet.description:
Many amateur radio operators believe that living in an HOA means giving up HF communication altogether.

But that simply isn't true.

In this video, we explore practical ways to operate amateur radio under HOA restrictions using antennas that blend naturally into their surroundings.

We'll look at stealth wire antennas, decorative flagpole antennas, magnetic loops, rain gutter antennas, portable operation, and other techniques that allow operators to stay active without large visible installations.

We'll also discuss an important concept that many operators overlook: antenna height in wavelengths, realistic expectations, and why the best antenna is often the one you can actually put on the air.

Whether you're a new ham, a portable operator, or someone trying to make the most of a restricted property, this video will help you understand the trade-offs and possibilities available.

73 and thanks for watching!

snippet.title: Electrician License Exam — What's On It and Why People Fail
snippet.description:
In 2024, only 27% of people passed the Texas journeyman electrician exam on their first attempt. The national average isn't much better, hovering between 40% to 60%. Why? Because the exam doesn't test how well you do the work—it tests your code-lookup speed and your ability to navigate the National Electrical Code (NEC) under intense time pressure.

In this video, we break down exactly what is on the electrician license exam, the 5 most common reasons experienced apprentices fail, and a step-by-step 8-week electrician exam study guide to guarantee you pass on your first try.

⚠️ IMPORTANT: Always verify your state’s current NEC edition (2020, 2023, or 2026) with your local licensing board before buying any study materials!

⚡ RESOURCES & STUDY TOOLS MENTIONED:
NEC Cheatsheet & Reference Guide: https://tinyurl.com/NECcheatsheet

### 📺 WATCH THE REST OF THE SERIES:

📍 STATE-BY-STATE LICENSING GUIDES:
*   How to Become an Electrician in Texas (The 16-Year-Old Loophole): https://youtu.be/gZPx47Jh2mE
*   How to Become an Electrician in California (The System Nobody Explains): https://youtu.be/q0yQrybOr14
*   How to Become a Licensed Electrician in Florida (2026): [Insert Link if active, or use this format]

⚡ ELECTRICIAN CAREER & LICENSE BASICS:
*   Apprentice vs Journeyman vs Master Electrician — What's the Real Difference: https://youtu.be/tHhQqfWCe6k
*   Do This Instead to Become an Electrician (Step-by-Step Guide): https://youtu.be/fPGw20Dw0UQ

🛠️ COMPARING THE TRADES (Which Path is Best?):
*   HVAC, Electrician, or Plumber? (Which Actually Pays More?): https://youtu.be/2IZotfoq73s
*   Best Trades to Get Into (No Experience, No Degree) [Part 1]: https://youtu.be/y629rJJCOOc
*   Best Trades to Get Into (No Experience, No Degree) [Part 2]: https://youtu.be/8L-G_k-1gbQ
*   AI is Destroying Office Jobs. Do This Instead: https://youtu.be/cXPxQA2C_aY

---

⏱️ TIMESTAMPS:
0:00 — The Brutal Truth About Journeyman Exam Pass Rates

0:35 — The Field-to-Code Gap (Why Great Electricians Fail)

1:12 — What's Actually Tested on the NEC Exam?

2:10 — The 5 Most Heavily Tested NEC Articles

2:35 — Calculation Formulas You Must Master

3:00 — 5 Mistakes That Will Guarantee a Fail

5:30 — The Ultimate 8-Week Passing Study Plan

7:15 — Exam Day Tips & Trick Options

📘 WHAT YOU WILL LEARN:
How to navigate the NEC in under 60 seconds using a strategic tabbing and indexing system.

Why Articles 210, 250, 300, 310, and 430 make up over 50% of your entire exam.

How to handle calculation pressure (voltage drop, demand factors, box fill, and conduit fill) without running out of time.

Why practicing with at least 500 questions is the magic number for first-time pass rates.

snippet.title: Got Your Ham License? Here's Why You Still Won't Key Up
snippet.description:
Free Video Course - The Top 5 Beginner Ham Radio Frustrations (and How To Overcome Them): https://hamradiolaunchpad.com/free

You passed the test. You bought the radio. And then you just… didn't. The mic sat there. The club meeting came and went. Weeks turned into months, and that callsign never left your wallet.

If that's you, you're not broken and you're not alone. This is the part of ham radio nobody warns you about — the moment you realize everyone else sounds like they know exactly what they're doing, and you're pretty sure you don't.

I sat in the back of my first club meeting convinced I didn't belong there. I had a brand new mobile radio in my car for three weeks and never keyed it once. This video is what I wish someone had told me then.

We'll get into:
— Why the fear feels bigger than the reality (and why that's normal)
— What was actually happening in that room while I was panicking
— The "slow" version of mic fright nobody talks about
— Three ways in — including the one that scares you most

You don't need to be fearless. You just need to be willing to be a beginner out loud.

📻 SUBSCRIBE
New videos every week — tutorials for new hams, plus Parks on the Road, our year-long RV trip activating 50+ parks across the country.

☕ SUPPORT THE CHANNEL
https://patreon.com/hamradiolaunchpad

💬 TELL ME YOURS
Have you walked into a club meeting and wanted to walk right back out? Sat with your thumb on the mic and never pressed it? Drop it in the comments. I read them all, and I promise you're in good company.

73,
Donnie (KD0JYV)

Timestamps:
1:24 — Why new hams stop before they start
1:55 — What the room was really like
3:24 — Three weeks with a radio I never used
4:51 — Mic fright doesn't always look like fear
6:23 — The grumpy expert who isn't there
7:24 — Where to start: parks, repeaters, clubs
8:34 — You're allowed to be a beginner

#hamradio #amateurradio #newham #micfright

snippet.title: How to Rebuild (Restuff) Vintage Paper and Molded Capacitors | Beginner Tutorial
snippet.description:
In this beginner-friendly tutorial, I'll show you how to rebuild (restuff) vintage paper and molded capacitors while preserving the original appearance of antique radios and other vintage electronic equipment while upgrading the internal component. 

Restuffing is one of the preferred restoration techniques used by museum restorers and experienced collectors because it keeps the chassis looking original while providing the safety and reliability of modern components.

🔧 Tools & Materials Used in This Restoration
As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases — at no extra cost to you.
These links help support the channel. Thank you!

• 👉 Aleene’s Original Tacky Glue:  https://amzn.to/4qlkX7I
• 👉 Peak Atlas LCR45:  https://amzn.to/4q4OpA3
• 👉 Precision Wire Cutter:  https://amzn.to/3Zmw8Sp
• 👉 Needle Nose Pliers (2-Pack, 6”):  https://amzn.to/4pIJ3ZW
• 👉 Rosin Core Solder:  https://amzn.to/3ZeHecn
• 👉 Soldering Flux:  https://amzn.to/3ZlwY1Z
• 👉 Foam Paint Brushes:  https://amzn.to/3PflR9r
• 👉 Clip-On Heat Sink:  https://amzn.to/4qV3Aeu

In this video you'll learn:
✔ What capacitor restuffing is
✔ Why rebuilding is better than simply replacing in valuable restorations
✔ The tools and materials you'll need
✔ Step-by-step rebuilding techniques for paper and molded capacitors
✔ How to install a modern polypropylene film capacitor inside the original shell
✔ Tips for achieving a factory-original appearance

Why rebuild instead of replace?
• Preserve the original appearance of the chassis.
• Help maintain the collector value of valuable vintage equipment.
• Enjoy the reliability and long life of modern polypropylene film capacitors.
• Reduce the risk of capacitor failure that can damage expensive components such as power transformers, audio transformers, vacuum tubes, and field coils.

Whether you're restoring your first antique radio or you're an experienced collector looking for authentic restoration techniques, this guide will help you achieve professional-looking results.

If you enjoy vintage radio restoration, be sure to Like, Subscribe, and turn on notifications so you don't miss future restoration projects, repair tutorials, and restoration tips.

Thanks for watching!

Regards from Florida,
Jay – Radio Fixer

snippet.title: Magic Kingdom Morning Adventure | Dapper Dans, PeopleMover & Parade | Day 319
snippet.description:
Day 319 became a full Saturday Morning Magic adventure at Magic Kingdom: sunrise, a guided meditation about trusting yourself again, Early Entry strategy, three Dapper Dans trolley performances, PeopleMover, Friendship Faire, two Adventure Friends Cavalcade passes, Magic Window surprises, the railroad and live Main Street piano. 🌅🏰

We began near Seven Seas Lagoon after a difficult pain day and slowed down for one important message:

You are allowed to trust yourself again.

Self-trust does not mean always knowing the right answer. Sometimes it means believing that whatever comes next, you will stay with yourself and take one step at a time.

From there we walked through the Polynesian and Grand Floridian resort areas, entered Magic Kingdom and built the morning around calm, practical park tips and spontaneous Disney moments.

Highlights included the sunrise, monorail fun, castle views, an Early Entry shortcut and rope-drop walkthrough, Let the Magic Begin, three Dapper Dans sets, PeopleMover, a mental-health check-in, Dapper Danny, Friendship Faire, two Adventure Friends/Vamos parade passes, Magic Window jump scares, a Cast Member pulling a heart from his pocket, Walt Disney World Railroad, a huge Haunted Mansion queue, Casey’s Corner piano and an approaching storm.

A huge thank-you to every Cast Member, performer, character attendant, entertainment coordinator, attraction and transportation team, food-service and custodial Cast Member, and behind-the-scenes team member who helped create this morning.

Special thanks to the Dapper Dans and their trolley team, the Adventure Friends Cavalcade performers and flag bearers, the characters and attendants who made the Magic Window interactions so much fun, Danny, the railroad team and the Main Street piano performers.

snippet.title: Strike Eagle  Fly Without License #drone #velocidrone #automobile #helicopter  #aviation #fpvdrone
snippet.description:
The M235 Strike Eagle is a powered ultralight aircraft built to meet FAA Part 103 rules. It requires no pilot license, no medical certificate, and no aircraft registration. This script explains the aircraft’s design, safety systems, controls, and cost advantages.

FAA Part 103 Basics
To qualify as an ultralight, an aircraft must stay under 254 pounds, fly no faster than 55 knots, stall no higher than 24 knots, carry one person, and operate only during the day.
The Strike Eagle is designed within all of these limits.

Airframe and Structure
The aircraft uses a TIG‑welded 6061 or 7020 aluminum frame.
A Kevlar crash cage surrounds the pilot for impact protection.
Load‑path transition plates sit between the motor mounts and the chassis to spread thrust and vibration loads and reduce stress on the frame.
The pilot sits in a Sparco Sprint seat with double‑locking sliders.

Wing System
The Strike Eagle uses an 80‑square‑foot composite wing with an I‑beam spar and the Selig S1223 airfoil.
With the motors off, the wing naturally settles into a 28‑knot passive stall, giving predictable glide behavior.

Power System
The aircraft uses eight BLDC motors, each with its own ESC for redundancy.
Power comes from two 52‑volt 14S8P battery packs wired in parallel and enclosed in a fireproof cover.

Flight Control System
There are two independent computers:

FC‑B (Pilot Brain): handles stabilization and normal flight control.

FC‑A (Safety Brain): monitors tilt, altitude, battery health, motor status, and FC‑B. If FC‑A detects a problem, it overrides FC‑B.

This separation improves safety and reduces pilot workload.

Pilot Controls
The pilot controls yaw, pitch, and roll with the right hand using a simple analog control stick.
Thrust is controlled with the left hand using an analog throttle.
Both analog inputs feed into a digital central controller, which interprets the signals and manages the motors through FC‑B.

This keeps the aircraft easy to fly while maintaining digital stability and safety oversight.

Emergency System
A whole‑aircraft ballistic parachute is included for emergency situations.

Safety Features
The Strike Eagle includes:

Kevlar crash cage

Passive stall wing

Ballistic parachute

Dual‑brain FC‑A / FC‑B architecture

Eight‑motor redundancy

Cost Comparison
General aviation is expensive.
A pilot license costs $12,000–$18,000, aircraft rental is $150–$200 per hour, annual maintenance is $5,000–$10,000, and certified aircraft cost $40,000–$120,000.
FAA‑certified engines also require mandatory overhauls every 1,800–2,000 hours, costing $25,000–$70,000.

The Strike Eagle avoids all of these costs.
No license, no medical, no registration, no fuel, no hangar, no certified engine, and no FAA‑mandated engine overhauls.
The aircraft itself costs less than a used Honda Civic.

Flight Experience
Flying the Strike Eagle is simple.
The pilot uses basic analog controls, while FC‑B manages stabilization.
The wing provides passive lift, and the motors provide vertical and forward thrust.

Support
A complete design package will be available for guidance, including diagrams, explanations, stress analysis, and a corresponding e‑book.
It will be available on Amazon and Gumroad for anyone who needs structured build support.

Closing
The MT235 Strike Eagle is a safe, simple, and affordable Part 103 ultralight that avoids the high costs of traditional aviation.
It offers an accessible way to experience personal flight.

Thank you for watching.

snippet.title: Licensed Mechanic Reacts: BMW Wants You To Pay Monthly For Heated Seats
snippet.description:
Can manufacturers charge you every month for features that are already installed in your vehicle?

BMW already experimented with subscription-based heated seats, and as a licensed mechanic, I think this opens the door to a much bigger conversation.

If automakers can charge monthly for heated seats today...

What's stopping subscriptions for horsepower, remote start, four-wheel drive, or other vehicle features tomorrow?

snippet.title: SOTA W7A/CO-133 Tombstone Hills
snippet.description:
This summit was activated on September 14, 2025. 

The summit is located just south of Tombstone on State Route 80, it is directly west of the airport. 

I have left the cw contacts essentially unedited. I did remove long breaks, random stuff and long strings of "CQ" and other boring stuff. If for some reason anyone that might watch these videos wants all the unedited stuff, let me know in the comments and I will put up some other videos of just raw contacts.

I didn't include code translation because I didn't think it would actually matter, you either know code or you don't. If that translation is something you are interested, let me know in the comments and I will add it.

snippet.title: Ham Radio on a Budget: FREE Listening + Kits Under £50 (Beginner Guide 2026)
snippet.description:
Getting into ham radio doesn't have to cost a fortune — this video covers every genuinely low-cost route in, from an affordable entry-level licence to free listening stations, low-cost kits, and the digital voice apps nobody tells beginners about.

We cover the real costs (and a few tongue-in-cheek "insider" tricks that genuinely happen) — entry-level licence fees vary by country but are more affordable than most people assume — plus free WebSDR listening, club remote stations, low-cost kit builds like the QMX, Chinese clone kits, satellite operating on an affordable handheld, and what to watch out for when buying second-hand gear that's been run hard on FT8.

Timestamps:
00:00 Intro and explanation
02:26 Low cost exam entry fee
03:21 Free access stations
05:21 Your local club
06:31 Kits
10:27 Boat anchors & the second hand market
11:37 Low cost HTs & free digital voice apps
14:20 Satellite operating
14:42 Antennae
15:15 Low cost kits and clones

20:36 End

snippet.title: Nothing Happened. It Was Perfect. | SOTA in the Gorce
snippet.description:
Yesterday it rained, and the cows called it. Today, nothing went wrong at all - and that turned out to be exactly what we wanted.

This was the morning after our Beskid Wyspowy climb, and the trail was still muddy from yesterday's rain. But the sky had completely changed. Sun out, no clouds, no cows lying down anywhere. The plan was simple: reach the tower on Gorc Kamieniecki (1228 m) and get on the air. We moved so much better than the day before. Legs warmed up, weather on our side, the whole family finding its rhythm on the trail. We reached the tower, I ran the activation, and then we took a slow break by a little wooden cottage with one of those views you just sit and look at for a while. And then we walked back down.

That's the whole story. No drama, no equipment trouble, no rain to beat. Just a good eight-hour day in the mountains with the people I like most - nothing to add, nothing to take away. Some of the best days are exactly like that.

SOTA (Summits on the Air) is a program where licensed amateur radio operators activate mountain summits around the world, making contacts from the top. Some activations are stories. This one was just a really good day.

This is Family on the Air - FOTA SOTA. Two summits, two very different days, back to back.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
📍 Location: Gorc Kamieniecki (1228 m), Gorce, Poland
🔖 SOTA Reference: SP/BZ-010
📻 Callsign: HF2LT
🌐 Summits on the Air: sotl.as
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Sometimes the mountains hand you a perfect day and ask for nothing back.

#SOTA #SummitsOnTheAir #HamRadio #FamilyOnTheAir #FOTASOTA #Poland #AmateurRadio #Gorce #FamilyAdventure

snippet.title: Ten Tec Omni VI Plus SSB CW HF transceiver dim display fault Ham Radio repair troubleshooting
snippet.description:
Ten Tec Omni VI Plus. Donated to D-Lab by N8CC (Jeff). He told me the radio was tired. Needed some TLC to get it back up & running. Back about 10 years ago, I had a VI, Opt 3. It was  a great CW rig, full of filters. I regretted getting rid of it. But thats what us Hams do. Always in search of the perfect rig. Well, I gave this one a going thru, found a few bad caps. Spent time troubleshooting the dim LED display. Found that it was just naturally going dim from age. No big deal, its still plenty readable. I have been operating it for about 2 weeks now, made many POTA contacts. Getting great signal reports. Have a mic & matching power supply on the way. I plan to keep this one. Now just need some room on the bench!
BTW: These transceivers are very nicely laid out, easy to work on. If you want the CW sport car, find an OMNI VI. Ten Tec sure had a good thing going. Miss them.
 My shop specializes on vintage home stereo, amplifier, guitar amp, and Ham radio repair. If you have a unique project, contact me. see: d-labelectronics.com or look me up on Patreon. If you like this info and would like to throw D-lab a bone, see: buymeaglassofwine.com
See: https://www.patreon.com/c/dlab_electronics

snippet.title: Strike Eagle Fly Ultralight, No License Needed! #drone #velocidrone  #aviation #fpv #helicopter #dji
snippet.description:
In a world where aviation is locked behind certificates, medicals, registrations, and bureaucracy… one aircraft breaks free.
This is the M235 Strike Eagle — a fully compliant FAA Part 103 powered ultralight, engineered to deliver safe, legal, personal flight with no pilot license required.

The Strike Eagle was built for one mission:
Give everyday people access to flight — without the barriers.  
Under Part 103, the rules are simple:
No pilot certificate
No medical certificate
No aircraft registration
No N‑number
No airworthiness certificate

Just you, the sky, and a machine designed to meet every requirement in the FAA ultralight rulebook.
At the heart of the Strike Eagle is a 23‑pound TIG‑welded 6061‑T6 or (7020) aluminum frame.
Every tube, every weld, every gusset is optimized for strength‑to‑weight — because Part 103 demands an empty weight under 254 pounds.

This frame is the backbone of the aircraft, carrying the propulsion system, the wing, the tail, and the pilot — all while staying inside the ultralight envelope.

The Strike Eagle uses eight coaxial BLDC motors, each paired with its own ESC.
This isn’t just power — it’s redundancy.
If one motor fails, seven remain.
If one ESC drops out, the others compensate.

At the core of the avionics is a dual‑flight‑computer architecture — FC‑A and FC‑B.
Two independent brains.
Two independent sensor suites.
Exactly the kind of safety configuration the FAA expects for powered ultralight operations.

Power flows from a four‑parallel 14S8P 52‑volt lithium packs, engineered for high discharge, thermal stability, and safe operation.
Mounted low in the frame, the battery anchors the center of gravity — a deliberate design choice that improves passive stability in both hover and forward flight.

The Strike Eagle’s wing is built around the legendary S1223 high‑lift airfoil — one of the most efficient low‑speed airfoils ever tested.

With a 24‑foot span and a 3‑foot chord, the wing has an area of 81 square feet.  For the M235 Strike Eagle with a 24 ft Span and S1223.

That’s the magic number.
It delivers a 24‑knot power‑off stall at 450 pounds gross weight, exactly matching the FAA’s maximum stall requirement for Part 103.

But the Strike Eagle isn’t just stable under power — it’s stable with the motors off.

A carefully tuned dihedral angle gives the wing a natural self‑righting effect.
If a wing drops, the geometry generates restoring lift that brings the aircraft back to level flight.

The center of gravity is positioned forward of the aerodynamic center, producing a gentle, predictable nose‑down glide instead of a stall break.

And trim tabs on the elevator allow the pilot to set a hands‑off glide attitude — letting the Strike Eagle descend smoothly, even in a complete power loss.

These passive features work together to create a stable glide mode, giving the pilot time, control, and confidence during unpowered descent.

Stability continues at the rear of the aircraft.
A carbon fiber tailplane, built as a flat‑plate horizontal stabilizer with a 10‑foot span and a 1.6‑foot chord, provides pitch authority and smooth control response.

Yaw control is handled by the vertical fin, 4 feet tall with a 1.4‑foot chord, mounted on a twin‑boom tail.
This configuration gives long moment arm, low drag, and excellent directional stability — without the weight of a single oversized fin.

Every element of the Strike Eagle is engineered around the FAA’s Part 103 ultralight rules:

Empty weight under 254 pounds
Maximum full‑power level flight speed under 55 knots CAS
Maximum power‑off stall speed under 24 knots CAS
Single‑seat only
Daylight operations only
No clouds
No Class A/B/C/D airspace without ATC authorization
No flying over congested areas or open‑air assemblies of people

This isn’t just an aircraft — it’s a machine built to fit perfectly inside the FAA’s simplest, most accessible category of flight.

And here’s where the Strike Eagle truly changes the game.

A traditional General Aviation pilot license costs between $15,000 and $20,000 in Southern California — before you ever touch an airplane of your own.
Aircraft rental, fuel, instructor fees, ground school, written tests, medical exams… the costs stack up fast.

But building the M235 Strike Eagle costs between $3,800 and $6,800 for a complete Part 103‑legal ultralight.
No license.
No medical.
No registration.
No annual inspections.
Just a one‑time build cost — and you’re flying.

The Strike Eagle isn’t just an aircraft.
It’s a fraction of the cost of traditional aviation, with none of the barriers.

Flying the Strike Eagle is unlike anything else.
You lift vertically on eight coaxial motors… transition smoothly into forward flight… and cruise under 55 knots with the stability of a fixed‑wing aircraft.

It’s quiet.
It’s efficient.
And thanks to its passive stability features, it’s safe — even with the motors off.

And it’s legal — no license required.

snippet.title: A conversa mais estranha da história 📻 #curiosidades
snippet.description:
Uma conversa em Código Morse pode parecer apenas uma sequência de bipes para quem nunca aprendeu esse sistema.

No entanto, operadores experientes desenvolvem a habilidade de reconhecer padrões sonoros completos, sem precisar traduzir cada letra individualmente.

Com bastante prática, o cérebro passa a identificar palavras e frases quase instantaneamente, permitindo conversas em alta velocidade.

Uma das formas de comunicação mais fascinantes já criadas e que ainda é utilizada por radioamadores e entusiastas ao redor do mundo.

#shorts #codigomorse #morse #radioamador #comunicacao #curiosidades #tecnologia #historia #viral

snippet.title: Bitcoiner’s Guide to Getting a US Ham Radio License (Remastered 2026): By HR4BTC
snippet.description:

Chapters:
00:00 Introduction  
00:07 HR4BTC's Mission Statement
00:44 Topics
01:08 License Types
01:30 Technician Class License
02:32 General Class License
03:23 Amateur Extra Class License
04:05 Test Preparation
05:55 Registering with the FCC
07:07 Finding a Test Site  
07:43 Taking the Test
09:04 After the Test
09:42 Outro

HR4BTC npub: ⁨npub1ya20ep3dd0qt0suhzprxztv5y43arqwps73ercvqa44sp7qw0edsp4x9ty⁩
https://primal.net/p/nprofile1qqszw48usckkhs9hcwt3q3np9k2z2c73s8qc0gu3uxqw66cqlq88ukc7rpwdk

Mission Statement:
Our mission is to bring Bitcoin Plebs & Devs into ham radio in order to help the bitcoin community achieve technical proficiency in radio communication techniques so that they will be able to contribute to advancing the radio arts in alignment with the ethos of Bitcoin

1. Use HamStudy.org:
HamStudy is a free website that provides practice tests for all ham radio licenses. It can help individuals prepare for their exams and find online or in-person test sessions.

www.hamstudy.org 

Check Out W4EEY on YouTube:
W4EEY on YouTube offers excellent ham radio tutorials. He has playlists covering all ham radio license levels and occasionally conducts live classes. This resource can be valuable for learning ham radio concepts.

Here are the playlists for the various licenses:

• Tech Playlist:
https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLYIFVi1uA3jGGEniNTxwsRKLUO55z9uO3

• General Playlist:
https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLYIFVi1uA3jE2WIuosncC4v-CQyAj9XTv

• Extra Playlist:
https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLYIFVi1uA3jFzzSqPFPHKBeeig9StFmIN

Become an ARRL VE:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=306mfbqHtLU

Explore ARRL and Local Clubs:
The American Radio Relay League (ARRL) website can help individuals find local ham radio clubs. These clubs often have information about local repeaters, club meetings, and ham radio "nets" (radio meetups). It's a good way to connect with other ham radio enthusiasts in your area.   (https://www.arrl.org/find-a-club)

Test Prep Links:
www.HamStudy.org
https://www.youtube.com/@W4EEY/playlists

Register with FCC for FRN:
https://apps.fcc.gov/cores

Find a Test Site:
https://hamstudy.org/sessions
https://www.arrl.org/find-an-amateur-radio-license-exam-session
https://www.arrl.org/find-a-club
